scene: Truly this was the Son of God
title: The Centurion Beneath the Cross
narration: I stand at my post, the ninth hour, and I watch a man die.
His eyes are sinking. The light in them dims, goes faint.
His face goes pale. The blood is spent, drained out of him.
His strength fails. He is at the edge of death now.
I have guarded many crosses. I know how men end here.
They cannot cry out. They can barely breathe. A thin word, then nothing.
So I brace myself for the quiet rattle, the slow giving-up.
But his head turns. Toward the woman below. A last farewell with no voice for it.
Then toward the sky, as a son looking to a father.
And he does not slump. He does not sag like the broken do.
He gathers himself. He lifts a great cry.
A voice loud and strong — Pater, in manus tuas — into the day.
It tears the air. I feel the ground move under my feet.
No dying man makes that sound. No man near death has that breath in him.
This cry is not nature's. It cannot be. Nature cannot do this.
And the head bows — calmly, as one chosen, not seized.
He lets the spirit go. He is not torn from life. He releases it.
Who lays down his garment when he wills? He sheds his flesh when he wills.
Who sleeps when he chooses? He died when he chose.
I have seen the power of a man dying. I tremble at what the Judge will be.
The word breaks out of me before I can hold it.
Truly — truly this was the Son of God.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who at the ninth hour hung upon the gibbet,
and crying with a loud voice commended your spirit into the Father's hands,
and with bowed head gave up that same spirit:
commend my spirit to yourself, now and always.
Pierce my heart with the sword of your love,
press into me the wounds of your body,
and at the end of my life set my spirit, given into your hands, among the blessed. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Vox ergo illa non fuit naturalis, sed miraculosa.
latinJewel.english: That voice, therefore, was not natural, but miraculous.
